summary: Gruber argues that the FTC's lawsuit against Amazon over Prime sign-up and cancellation practices is weak and possibly frivolous. He contends that canceling Prime is actually easier than most subscriptions, and that the extra confirmation steps are reasonable safeguards rather than dark patterns. He pointedly notes that publications like the Wall Street Journal and New York Times have far more egregious cancellation practices. He suggests the lawsuit reflects FTC chair Lina Khan's personal crusade against Amazon rather than genuine consumer harm, and advocates instead for broad regulation requiring all subscription services to offer clear online cancellation.
key_points: The FTC's case that Amazon makes Prime hard to cancel fails the real-world test — Gruber has never heard anyone complain about it, unlike cable TV, gyms, or newspaper subscriptions
Extra confirmation steps before cancellation are reasonable 'Are you sure?' safeguards, comparable to emptying the Trash on a computer, not dark patterns
The Wall Street Journal reporting on Amazon's cancellation practices while itself requiring phone calls to cancel subscriptions is deeply ironic
The FTC should pursue broad regulation requiring all subscription services to have clear online cancellation rather than singling out Amazon
The lawsuit appears driven by Lina Khan's well-documented personal focus on Amazon rather than by the severity of the consumer harm
money_quotes: I find the FTC's case against Amazon to be weak sauce at best, and bordering on frivolous.
I've never once heard of anyone complaining that they were tricked into a Prime membership that they didn't knowingly sign up for.
It's way easier to cancel a Prime membership than most subscriptions.
It's like complaining that it takes a few more steps to empty the Trash in MacOS than it does to create a new file or folder — destructive actions should take a few extra steps.

I'd be all in favor of the FTC pursuing and enforcing laws that require all subscription services to have clear, discoverable cancellation options online. In fact, it seems ridiculous that the FTC hasn't already done this.
But singling out Amazon only makes sense insofar as that Lina Khan made a name for herself as a critic of the company.

The Wall Street Journal's story on the lawsuit, which — shocker, I know — somehow doesn't mention that The Wall Street Journal itself requires subscribers to make a phone call to cancel a digital subscription to the Journal.
key_insight: The FTC's case against Amazon Prime is undermined by the fact that far worse subscription cancellation practices exist unchallenged across the industry, suggesting the lawsuit is more about Lina Khan's anti-Amazon agenda than genuine consumer protection.
